title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
text:
Time

Wow, it’s already five!








True or false?

title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
text:
More examples

Context: Entering a large movie theater with two people seated.  “The theater is empty.”



Context: You get out of the shower and dry yourself off with a towel, but you miss one droplet.  “Okay, I’m dry.”

title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
text:
Imprecision

Imprecise (uses of) expressions: a little like vague expressions, e.g., tall.

The Empire State Building is tall.


tall doesn’t let us know exactly how tall the ESB is.
Sort of like dry doesn’t always let us know exactly how dry someone is.

title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
text:
Sorites

But imprecision is different.

Sorites paradox:


The ESB is tall.
A building 1 mm shorter than a tall building is tall.
Therefore, a one-story house is tall.


Compare:


A theater with no people in it is empty.
A theater with one more person in it than an empty theater is empty.

title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
text:
Shrinking/widening the halo
Slack regulators shrink or widen the halo.

Widening: Wow, it’s already about five!
Shrinking: Wow, it’s already exactly five!
Shrinking: The theater is completely empty.

title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
text:
Changing the standard of precision
The standard of precision (SOP) can change (Lewis (1979)).

Jo: It’s already five.      

Bo: No, it’s 4:58!

Jo: The theater is empty. 

Bo: No, someone’s sitting in the corner!

Jo: Okay, I’m dry. 

Bo: No, you have a few drops on your arm.

title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
text:
Unidirectionality
Compare:

Jo: It’s already five. Bo: No, it’s 4:58! 

Jo: # Okay, but it’s still five!

Jo: The theater is empty. Bo: No, someone’s in the corner. 

Jo: # Sure, but it’s empty.

Jo: Okay, I’m dry. Bo: No, you have a few drops on your arm. 

Bo: # Sure, but I’m dry.



↝ Only possible to shrink the pragmatic halo?

title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
text:
Plan

Test of unidirectionality (Wu and Aparicio, newer work)
Develop two statistical models of Wu and Aparicio’s data

Model 1: uses the probabilistic structure of pragmatic halos
Model 2: regards imprecision as a kind of vagueness

Compare models using standard model comparison metrics (ELPD)

title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
text:
Testing unidirectionality
Wu and Aparicio collect judgments about the application of adjectives like empty.



(They look at S1, S4, and S5.)

title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
text:
Three blocks
Experimental participants were told that they’d interact with someone (in reality, a bot). The bot might disagree with them.

title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
text:
Two kinds of bot
You could be paired with either an imprecise bot (Experiment 1) or a precise bot (Experiment 2).

title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
text:
Experiment 1 (imprecise bot)




  


Disagreement seems to lower the standard (a little)!

title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
text: Precise bot: dialogue perception

title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
text:
Summary

A precise bot raises the SOP.
But an imprecise bot can lower it (a little)!
We have:

bidirectionality
bias toward raising the SOP/shrinking the pragmatic halo

title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
text:
Modeling pragmatic halos




Hypothesis: biased bidirectionality arises from our probabilistic knowledge about pragmatic halos.

Higher standards of precision are more likely;
but lower standard of precision are still possible.

title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
text:
Detour: scale structure

Maximum standard absolute adjectives: empty, full, clean

these can be used imprecisely

Relative adjectives: tall, wide, long

these are vague



This difference is reflected in scale structure.

title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
text:
Two models
Absolute model: there is an upper endpoint on the scale, and we model the SOP as departing from this endpoint.

title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
text:
Two models
Relative model: there is no upper endpoint - the adjectival standard is vague.

title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
text:
Schematically…

We design Bayesian hierarchical mixed effects models.
Ordered logistic likelihoods
Acceptance of the adjective and judgments about the dialogue are predicted using:

parameters sensitive to adjectival scale structure;
probabilities computed in terms of these parameters.

We fit both models to Block 1 only, as well jointly to all three blocks.

title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
text:
Model comparisons
Expected log pointwise predictive density (ELPD).

Measures goodness of fit to data.
Penalizes model complexity.
Estimates out-of-sample performance.


ELPD difference (s.e. of the difference):

title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026
text:
Summary and future
We have some preliminary evidence that the apparent unidirectionality of precisification arises from:

lexical meaning (adjectival scale structure), combined with
prior probabilistic knowledge about pragmatic halos.


Ongoing:

We need similar data involving vague (rather than imprecise) adjectives. This would:
test the prediction that the relative model would perform better than the absolute model in this case.

title: Adjectival scale structure and the discourse dynamics of imprecision  Julian Grove (U. Florida)  April 15 (tax day), 2026

References




Lasersohn, Peter. 1999. “Pragmatic Halos.” Language 75 (3): 522–51. https://doi.org/10.2307/417059.


Lewis, David. 1979. “Scorekeeping in a Language Game.” In Semantics from Different Points of View, edited by Rainer Bäurele and Arnim von Stechow, 6:172–87. Springer Series in Language and Communication. Berlin: Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-642-67458-7_12.


Wu, Yifan, and Helena Aparicio. 2025. “Disagreements Do Not Automatically Raise the Standard of Precision.” Experiments in Linguistic Meaning 3 (January): 435–46. https://doi.org/10.3765/elm.3.5835.
